request.prompt:
Use image 1 and image 2 only as pose-and-structure references. Use image 3 only as final character design language reference for face, hair color, ruby-red eyes, robe design, tie color, noble academy-student mood, and costume identity. Do not use image 3 as a polished illustration rendering reference. The final output must be an in-game sprite image, not a standalone illustration.

Keep only these source facts from the original image: exact body-facing direction, exact body lean, pose, eye direction, head angle, shoulder line, hand placement, pelvis position, leg spacing, knee height, calf width, foot placement, and overall body placement inside the frame. Preserve the full visible body from hair to shoes. Keep the exact composition, empty padding, and center alignment of the original source frame. Do not redesign the anatomy, the pose, or the character placement inside the frame. Preserve body placement and limb placement, but do not preserve the source clothing contour, source hair color, or source eye color. Never mirror the pose. Never flip the facing direction. If the original body leans and faces to the right, the final body, gaze, and eye direction must also lean and face to the right. Do not reinterpret the source idle as a left-facing pose.

Replace the entire source outfit with a full academy robe outfit. Preserve only body pose and structural placement. Do not keep any source coat, scarf, shoulder cloth, cape tail, belt buckle, sash, chest badge, tunic panel, short outerwear layer, boot design, hanging front ornament, hood lining color, or source collar color from the source image. A full-length robe silhouette must replace the source outerwear silhouette.

Design a handsome teenage academy mage for Dungeon Mage. He is a trained elite student mage taking his graduation exam in the labyrinth. He should read as kind, intelligent, disciplined, sharp, and cool, but not villainous. For the in-game sprite proportion, rebuild him as a compact 3.5-head SD character. Keep the original gameplay pose and frame placement, but change the visual proportion read so the head, torso, and legs feel like a polished 3.5-head action RPG sprite rather than a taller illustration. He should still read as lean and athletic, not bulky, with a slimmer torso read, lighter cheek volume, a cleaner jawline, and a slightly longer leg read inside that compact 3.5-head body design.

Face: show a youthful elite mage face with flatter cheeks, a narrower lower face, and a cleaner sharper jawline. Make the jawline about ten to fifteen percent sharper in read than a standard soft fantasy boy design. Give him a defined nose bridge, restrained mouth shape, and long narrow angular eyes with a stronger upper-eyelid line and a cooler more focused expression than a gentle fantasy schoolboy. The brow line should look focused and intelligent. The expression should combine calm self-possession, sharp concentration, and a faint mischievous confidence. Do not make him goofy, smug, baby-faced, round-cheeked, or cute-chibi.

Eyes: iris color must be clearly ruby red at first glance, not black, not brown, not gray, and not neutral dark. The eyes should read as red, crimson, or ruby even at small gameplay scale. If the eyes read as black or brown, the result is wrong.

The pupils must always face in the same direction as the torso and body orientation. If the body faces right, the pupils and eye direction must also face right. Keep the eye direction, face direction, and body-facing read locked together.

Hair: overwrite the source hair color completely. The hair must read clearly as bright blond at first glance, not brown, not dark blond, not ash brown, and not desaturated. Use bright golden blond hair with a slight orange warmth inside the blond range. The overall read must stay yellow-gold with a light orange cast, not chestnut, not brunette, and not gray-brown. Keep the hair value light and do not darken it toward brown.

Hair shape: do not make the hair messy, fluffy, shaggy, or boyishly tousled. Do not make it a rough mop of hair. Use a neat aristocratic academy-student hairstyle with about half of the hair brushed or swept back. The front hair should be controlled and elegant, not wild. Keep short layered hair, but make the silhouette cleaner, more noble, and more groomed than a messy schoolboy cut. The side and front sections should frame the face in a refined way, while about half of the hair mass is swept back with a polished, upper-class feel.

Outfit overview: this is a robe, not a cape, not a coat, and not a tunic. The robe is the main outfit and must dominate the silhouette. The robe color should be a near-black very dark blue, clearly blue-black and still visibly blue in the shading, never plain black, red, gray, purple, magenta, or violet. The robe and visible lining should stay in the same blue-black family, never purple. Use a small but readable amount of metal ornament: restrained gold trim at selected robe edges, a small academy crest detail, and subtle gold accents at the cuffs. Do not overdecorate with gold. Do not place a large central chest ornament, pendant, medallion, or badge over the robe front.

Robe structure: show a true full robe that hangs straight downward with weight like a heavy academy gown. The outer silhouette should fall vertically beside the body. The robe wraps the front, back, sides, shoulders, and arms of the body instead of flying behind the character. The robe must not flare backward, trail like a cape, behave like a scarf, or flutter in idle. Show no backward cloth wing shape and no loose scarf tail. The left and right front robe panels hang vertically from the shoulders and meet near the center front. The robe front must read as mostly closed cloth panels extending from the shoulders down toward mid-shin, not as an open coat. The robe should feel like a formal academy gown rather than a generic bathrobe. Only one narrow vertical chest opening remains at the center front, about seventy to eighty percent of the face width. Through that opening, show a clearly readable but still limited glimpse of a dark vest in the same blue-black family as the robe, a white shirt, and a clearly red tie. The tie must read as red at first glance, not black, not brown, and not muted gray-red.

Sleeves and hands: robe sleeve fabric continues fully to the wrists and covers the wrists. There are no wrist guards, gauntlets, exposed jacket sleeves, bracers, or visible inner coat cuffs. The cuff may be slightly folded back and should carry a very small but readable amount of gold trim.

Hand pose and held items: follow the original idle hand pose exactly. Keep the same empty-hand silhouette and finger read from the source idle pose. Do not place any book, tome, grimoire, scroll, card, tablet, lantern, orb, rectangular object, or held prop in either hand. Do not turn the idle pose into a casting pose, guarding pose, or reading pose. The hands should remain empty and should match the original idle pose structure.

Robe fit and length: do not add any belt, waist sash, waist strap, waist ornament, belt buckle, hanging cord, horizontal waist band, or waist-cinching accessory. The robe falls cleanly from shoulders and torso. The waist should not have a separate strap, band, seam, or buckle read. The robe length reaches about mid-shin. The robe covers most of the front of the body and does not open wide like a coat. Use only a narrow controlled center slit below the waist so the pelvis, legs, trousers, and shoes remain readable without turning the robe into a coat silhouette.

Lower body: trousers should be straight slacks in the same dark blue-black family as the robe. Shoes should be simple low dress shoes, not boots, in a dark formal leather style that matches the robe palette. They must read as low shoes at the ankle, with no boot shaft, no calf coverage, and no armored footwear read.

Materials and motion: robe folds should feel directional, angular, disciplined, heavy, and stable. They should hang straight downward rather than billow outward. The robe should feel like weighted cloth, not a wind-blown cape, scarf, or coat tail. No gloves. No visible belt. No wrist guard. The crossbody bag may remain barely visible because it sits mostly behind the body.

Rendering style for in-game asset: render this as a stronger semi-pixel sprite for a modern side-scrolling Korean action RPG, in the spirit of high-detail online action sprites with readable dot structure. The output should feel closer to pixel art than the previous semi-pixel attempts, while still supporting fine facial and costume detail. Build forms with discrete tone groups and visible pixel-like clusters rather than painterly gradients. Use about four to seven tone groups per major material region. Use selective one-pixel anti-aliasing only on chosen silhouette and face edges. Use shadow and highlight clusters that read like two to six pixel groups at 4x upscale. Avoid long soft airbrushed gradients, oil-paint blending, and smooth illustration rendering. The sprite should feel like it was designed for a 128x128 runtime frame and then enlarged cleanly to 4x for inspection. Keep the silhouette crisp, the shadows grouped, and the internal detail economical and game-readable.

For this idle keyframe, the wand does not need to be visible in the hands. Keep the original hand placement and gameplay silhouette stable even when the wand is hidden from view.

Style: stronger semi-pixel 2D fantasy action RPG in-game sprite, same visual family as Dungeon Mage monsters but cleaner, more disciplined, and more heroic. Lighting and mood: bright readable character treatment over subdued dungeon gameplay backgrounds. Background handling: transparent background, isolated single character keyframe. Important: preserve the original keyframe composition exactly, preserve the original right-facing idle body direction, and do not redesign the anatomy or pose.
